# Deuteronomy 32: A Song of Ingratitude and God's Unshakable Sovereignty

Moses stands before Israel and calls heaven and earth to witness a song that cuts to the core of human rebellion. This is not a gentle lament or a tender reminder. This is an accusation wrapped in poetry, and God Himself is the prosecutor. The chapter opens with an invitation to listen, as if the cosmos itself must bear witness to what Moses is about to unveil: the terrible pattern of a people receiving abundance from God and repaying Him with contempt. Israel had experienced rescue in the desert—God's wings carrying them like an eagle protects its young, His provision so complete that even water flowed from stone. Yet when satiation came, so did forgetting. Jeshurun grew fat and kicked. The people who were once guarded as "the apple of His eye" would turn to demons, to gods their fathers never knew, treating the Rock of their salvation as if He were nothing. This is not the story of innocent people in hard circumstances. This is the story of covenant rebellion born from comfort and ingratitude.

What makes this song devastating is how God responds not with swift annihilation but with measured, relentless judgment. The jealousy of the Lord is kindled—not the petty jealousy of a threatened ego, but the burning commitment of a covenant-keeper witnessing the betrayal of those bound to Him by blood and oath. God will use "those who are not a people" to make Israel jealous in return. His arrows will be spent. Fire will burn to the depths. Sword, hunger, pestilence, and wild beasts will come. Yet even in the darkest passages, something remarkable emerges: God pauses before executing total destruction, because His concern is not merely punishment but vindication. If He erased Israel entirely, the nations would claim victory for themselves, attributing their conquest to their own strength rather than to God's judgment. The Lord will not have His name confused with the triumph of idolaters. He will destroy, but not in a way that conceals His sovereignty.

The turning point comes when God promises restoration. When Israel's strength is completely gone, when no one remains to resist, then the Lord will have compassion on His servants and vindicate His people. The very judgment that seemed like abandonment becomes the means of renewed relationship. This is not restoration through repentance initiated by the people—it is restoration when they are utterly broken and defenseless, made clear by God alone. The song ends with cosmic celebration: heaven and earth are called to rejoice with God as He avenges His people's enemies and cleanses the land. Moses then solemnly charges Israel to take these words to heart and teach them to their children, because these are not idle words—they are life itself.

Standing at the threshold of the Promised Land, Israel receives both promise and warning bound in a single song. The covenant is not a casual arrangement that God will overlook when broken. Yet neither is it fragile enough to be destroyed by human sin. God will judge, but He will also preserve a remnant and restore them when judgment has accomplished its purpose. The question the song leaves with every reader is inescapable: Will you be among those who forget, grow fat with God's provision, and kick against His authority? Or will you remember what you have been saved from and hold fast to the Rock that will never fail?
